headline: Software and Pharma Find Shelter as Semis Crater on Geopolitical Shock
body_html:
Friday's session ended in a near-perfect split, with 99 advancers facing off against 100 decliners across our 200-name universe — a coin-flip breadth reading that masked a violent rotation underneath. The dominant theme was unmistakable: U.S. military strikes against Iran, and Tehran's retaliatory moves against American positions in the region, sent investors sprinting away from anything cyclically exposed and into the relative safety of software and healthcare. Energy sold off sharply despite firm diesel refining economics, while Materials and Industrials each shed more than two percent as infrastructure spending narratives wilted under the weight of geopolitical uncertainty.

Semiconductors bore the heaviest damage, tumbling 7.21% as a group. ON Semiconductor led all decliners with a stunning 23.66% collapse, followed by connectivity-chip maker Credo Technology at -11.2% and memory-adjacent names Western Digital and Sandisk shedding 13.17% and 10.46% respectively. The carnage suggested the market is pricing in meaningful supply-chain and demand disruption across the chip food chain. Bloom Energy's 18.49% plunge added fuel-cell pain to the energy complex.

The day's standout winner was Snowflake, which surged 9.65% to top the leaderboard, with Datadog and Zscaler not far behind at 8.52% and 6.83% — a clean sweep for cloud software. Eli Lilly and Biogen each rose more than 6.9%, extending the healthcare sector's recent momentum to fresh record territory. When geopolitics scrambles the macro picture this sharply, the market's instinct remains consistent: own recurring-revenue software and drug pipelines, and sell the hardware that sits closest to a disrupted supply chain.

briefs.headline: U.S.-Iran Exchanges Rattle Markets as Strait of Hormuz Tensions Flare
briefs.body: American forces struck Iran following an attack on a cargo vessel in the Strait of Hormuz, with Tehran's Revolutionary Guards announcing retaliatory strikes against U.S. regional positions. The back-and-forth injected fresh risk premium across energy and industrial names while pushing defensive sectors sharply higher. Diplomatic contacts, including a rare UAE-Iran call stressing Hormuz security, offered only modest reassurance.

briefs.headline: Chip Stocks Punished as Sector Sheds More Than Seven Percent
briefs.body: The semiconductor group endured its worst single-session showing of the year, falling 7.21% in aggregate. ON Semiconductor's 23.66% decline stood as the sharpest drop in the entire universe, with storage and connectivity names compounding the damage. Investors appear to be pricing in both demand uncertainty and potential supply-chain disruption tied to the escalating regional conflict.

briefs.headline: Healthcare Names Extend Record-Setting Run
briefs.body: All three healthcare holdings in our universe closed at all-time highs, pushing the sector to a 1.94% gain on the day. Eli Lilly advanced 7.13% while Biogen added 6.97%, with investors treating large-cap pharma as a haven from geopolitical noise. Debate over GLP-1 weight-loss drugs and employer insurance coverage adds a policy wrinkle to the sector's otherwise strong momentum.

briefs.headline: Snowflake Tops the Leaderboard With Nearly Ten Percent Jump
briefs.body: Snowflake's 9.65% gain made it the session's standout mover, capping a strong day for cloud-data infrastructure names. Datadog and Zscaler each rose more than six percent, signaling that enterprise software spending narratives remain intact even as macro uncertainty intensifies. The Software sector finished the day up 2.39%, the best-performing group in the universe.

briefs.headline: Oracle Posts Its Worst Weekly Stretch Since the Dot-Com Era
briefs.body: Oracle shares wrapped up their steepest weekly decline since the 2001 dot-com bust, as growing investor concern over artificial-intelligence financing weighed heavily on the stock. The selloff stands in contrast to the broad strength seen among other cloud-software names on Friday. It underscores how quickly sentiment can differentiate between AI infrastructure spending winners and those perceived as carrying execution risk.

briefs.headline: Israel-Lebanon Accord Adds Diplomatic Counterweight to Mideast Tension
briefs.body: Even as U.S.-Iran hostilities escalated, a separate U.S.-brokered preliminary agreement between Israel and Lebanon offered a partial diplomatic offset. The deal provided some comfort to investors monitoring broader Middle East stability, though energy markets remained under pressure. A UAE clarification that an earlier public warning stemmed from a technical malfunction also helped temper a brief spike in risk aversion during afternoon trading.
